flashcards.front: What is Block Coding in data communication?
flashcards.back: A technique that adds redundant bits to data blocks to enable error detection and correction. Original data is divided into fixed-size blocks, and redundant bits are added based on mathematical algorithms.

flashcards.front: Define Code Rate in block coding
flashcards.back: The ratio of data bits to total transmitted bits (including redundancy). Formula: Code Rate = k/n where k is data bits and n is codeword length.

flashcards.front: What is Minimum Distance (d_min) in block codes?
flashcards.back: The minimum Hamming distance between any two valid codewords. Determines error detection/correction capability: detects up to d_min-1 errors, corrects up to floor((d_min-1)/2) errors.
The number of positions at which two codewords of equal length differ. Used to measure how different two binary strings are. For example, 10110 and 11100 have Hamming distance 2.

flashcards.front: What is a Linear Block Code?
flashcards.back: A block code where the codeword space forms a linear vector space over GF(2). The sum of any two codewords is also a valid codeword. Includes parity-check and Hamming codes.
A vector computed by multiplying the received vector by the parity-check matrix. Syndrome = 0 means no error; non-zero syndrome indicates error pattern and helps locate errors.
Adds one parity bit to make the total number of 1s even (even parity) or odd (odd parity). Detects odd number of errors but cannot correct errors. Code rate = k/(k+1).
Data arranged in rows and columns, with parity bits added to each row and column. Can detect and correct single-bit errors by identifying the exact error position using row and column parity.
A linear block code that can single-bit errors. Uses redundant bits placed at positions that are powers of 2. For (n,k) code, relation is 2^r >= n+1 where r is parity bits.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between error detection and error correction?
flashcards.back: Error detection only identifies if errors occurred (using CRC, parity); receiver requests retransmission. Error correction locates and fixes errors without retransmission (requires stronger codes like Hamming).
A linear code with cyclic property: any cyclic shift of a codeword is also a valid codeword. Used in CRC, BCH codes. Easy to implement using shift registers.
The complete transmitted sequence consisting of original data bits (k) plus redundant parity bits (r). Total length n = k + r. Each valid combination forms a codeword.
A powerful error detection code using polynomial division. Data is divided by a generator polynomial, and remainder is appended as CRC bits. Detects burst errors up to length of generator polynomial.
The simplest block code where each bit is repeated n times. Can detect errors if majority voting works but has very low code rate (1/n). Example: sending 0 as 000, 1 as 111.
A decoding method where received vector is multiplied by parity-check matrix to get syndrome. Syndrome is matched with error pattern to locate and correct errors in the received codeword.
